title: Trusting God: God's Sovereignty
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: God’s Sovereignty - Trusting God - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Preparing to Study God's Sovereignty
subpoints: Chapter 3 of Jerry Bridges' Trusting God: the sovereignty of God
Chapters 4-7 will address sovereignty in specific areas (people, nations, nature, and human responsibility)
Two words of caution: the finite cannot comprehend the infinite (Turretin), and we should always assume Scripture is right before assuming we understand it
Encouragement: Romans 15:4 — Scripture is given for our learning, that we might have hope

outline.title: Point 1: God Is Absolutely in Control of All Things
subpoints: Previous chapters established: God is trustworthy, God is in control, God is wise and loving
Common objections: God limited himself in creating free will; things happen by chance
R.C. Sproul: if a single molecule is outside God's control, he cannot be fully trusted
2 Timothy 1:12: Paul suffers and yet trusts God to keep what he has committed to him
God ordains all things — the word 'permit' is acceptable only if it means 'ordains'; there are not two categories

outline.title: Point 2: God's Sovereignty Is Not Always Apparent
subpoints: We often cannot see God's hand at work in real time — he works behind the scenes
The Book of Esther: six seemingly coincidental events that together saved the Jewish people — none coincidental
The Book of Ruth: four ordinary events that preserved the Davidic line — nothing extraordinary, yet all ordained
God also works in circumstances that do not have 'happy endings' from our perspective (Amos 3:6; Eccl. 7:14; Isa. 45:7)
We should not shield God from being responsible — Amos 3:6 is still in the Bible

outline.title: Point 3: God Does What He Pleases, and No One Can Thwart Him
subpoints: Job 42:2; Psalm 115:3; Isaiah 14:27; 46:10; Daniel 4:35; Ephesians 1:11 — a survey of Scriptural testimony
If God's plan can be thwarted, our prayers are futile
His plan is to conform us to the image of his Son (Romans 8:29)
God's plan for Joseph — sold, imprisoned, forgotten — and yet it all served a redemptive purpose
Trusting God happens one event at a time, one circumstance at a time, sometimes one minute at a time

outline.title: Application: Trusting God in Adversity
subpoints: If bad things happen to us, it is because the Lord who loves us has ordained them for our good
God never says 'if only I had known' — he was not surprised by your cancer diagnosis or your job loss
Our typical response to adversity is 'how do I get out of this?' — but the better question is 'how do I trust God in this?'
Christ in Gethsemane: 'Not my will, but yours be done' — trusting sovereignty is an act of the will, not feelings
1 Thessalonians 5:18: give thanks in all circumstances — unthankfulness is a symptom of not trusting God's sovereignty

summary:
In this Sunday school lesson from the first week of a series on Jerry Bridges' book *Trusting God*, Elder Nick Anderson teaches on chapter three: the sovereignty of God. He introduces the series by noting that chapters 4-7 will address God's sovereignty in specific areas (people, nations, nature, and human responsibility), while this lesson lays the theological foundation. He opens with two words of caution: the finite cannot comprehend the infinite, and apparent contradictions in Scripture should lead us first to humility about our own understanding, not skepticism about the Word.

The lesson is organized around three main points. First, God is absolutely in control of all things — not merely things he 'permits,' but all things. Elder Anderson addresses the common objection that God limited himself in creating free will, citing R.C. Sproul: if a single molecule is outside God's control, he cannot be trusted at all. He surveys several Proverbs passages and Lamentations 3:37 to show that God's purposes stand and all things occur according to his decree — including suffering, adversity, and even evil acts, most decisively the crucifixion of Christ.

Second, God's sovereignty is not always apparent. Using the books of Esther and Ruth, the lesson shows how God orchestrates ordinary and extraordinary circumstances behind the scenes without any visible intervention. Both stories involve chains of seemingly coincidental events that together accomplish God's purposes — none of which were actually coincidental.

Third, God does what he pleases and no one can thwart him. A rapid survey of texts from Job, Psalms, Isaiah, Daniel, and Ephesians establishes this beyond doubt. The lesson closes with practical application: trusting God in adversity is not a matter of feelings but of the will — choosing to believe what we know about God even when circumstances seem to contradict it, just as Christ himself chose to trust his Father in Gethsemane.
transcript:
## Introduction

For our adult Sunday school today, I'll be teaching out of chapter three of the book that we're going through in our book study, which is *Trusting God*. And if you're not in a book group, it's not too late to join — I would encourage you to do so. My group has had encouraging discussions and it's a good time of fellowship, and it's a good book to read as well.

In chapters one and two, we laid the foundation of the book, and we saw that God is trustworthy and that God is in control. And in chapter three, we're going to be looking specifically at God's sovereignty. Now, before I pray, I'm just going to say one thing. Chapters four, five, six, and seven are also going to deal with God's sovereignty, but in specific instances — in specific areas of his sovereignty dealing with people, nations, nature, and God's sovereignty and our responsibility. So if you have questions about those four categories, you can save them for those chapters, and whoever teaches those chapters gets to answer those hard questions. But I'll answer any of your questions today regarding what I'm talking about.

Let's have a word of prayer.

Gracious God, we thank you that you are indeed God, and that you are sovereign over all the universe, over all things that come to pass, for you have ordained them, and you have ordained them for our good, because you are a good and loving God. And we pray, Lord, this morning, as we look at your sovereignty, that you would help us to have humble hearts and open minds to what your Word teaches us. We pray, Lord, that you would help us to believe all the things that you have given to us in your Word, for you have given them to us for our comfort and for our hope. And we pray that you would humble us as we look at your absolute sovereignty over all things. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.

## Two Words of Caution

So a couple of words of caution, and encouragement, as we look at God's sovereignty — because this can be a very difficult subject to understand and wrap our heads around. In fact, in some ways, we can't wrap our heads around this subject.

The first thing we need to do when we're talking about difficult things is to check our own attitude. Francis Turretin in his *Institutes of Elenctic Theology* says this — he's defending the use of reason against some who would say we shouldn't deduce things from Scripture — but he says this about reason's limits: *Reason must not be listened to when it complains of not being able to comprehend the mysteries of faith, for how can the finite comprehend the infinite?*

And an analogy that helped me: if we could somehow speak to a worm — a worm is a totally different type of creature than we are — and try to explain what we are, it might understand some things about us, but it could not understand everything, because we are a different type of being. In the same way, God is a different type of being than we are. When he speaks to us, he condescends to our understanding. He speaks to us in ways we can understand something about him — but we cannot understand everything, because he is a different type of being.

Turretin also says: *If we cannot find a way to reconcile an apparent contradiction — because there are no true contradictions in Scripture — it is always wiser to acknowledge our own ignorance than to assume Scripture is wrong.*

And lastly, an encouraging word: Romans 15:4 — *For whatever things were written before were written for our learning, that we through the patience and comfort of the Scriptures might have hope.* As we look at the doctrine of God's sovereignty, if it doesn't lead us to hope, that doesn't mean there's something wrong with the doctrine. It means there's something wrong with us and the way we're thinking about it.

## Point 1: God Is Absolutely in Control of All Things

The first point is that God is absolutely in control of all things. All things come to pass because God has ordained them to come to pass.

There are three things we learned in the previous chapters: first, we must trust that God is sovereign; second, we must trust God in times of adversity; and third, we must trust that God is wise and loving. Of these three things, the one that gets challenged most in our minds is the absolute sovereignty of God over all things.

Andrew Murray wrote one of the more common objections: *In creating man with a free will and making him a partner in the rule of the earth, God limited himself... Man by his prayer would hold the measure of what God could do in blessing.* That should strike you as wrong. Nothing biblical about what he just said.

Another argument: things just happen by chance, circumstances are outside our control. It's really a failure to acknowledge that God actually is God and that he actually ordains all things.

How do we respond to these arguments? First, we need to know what the Bible says about God's sovereignty. Second, we need to remember that the degree to which we believe God is sovereign will have a direct impact on how much we trust him. R.C. Sproul said: *If there is a single molecule that is outside of God's control — if there is a single event that can occur outside of God's sovereign control — then we cannot trust him at all.* He may love us infinitely and want the best for us, but if he lacks even one gram of power to control history, his purposes may fail for us. We may not make it to heaven because God is not in control.

Thankfully, that is not our God. Paul in 2 Timothy 1:12: *For this reason I also suffer these things. Nevertheless I am not ashamed, for I know whom I have believed and am persuaded that he is able to keep what I have committed to him until that day.* Paul is suffering — shipwrecked, beaten, cold, hungry, destitute — and yet he trusts God with his eternal salvation. If God is able to guarantee our eternal salvation, is he not able to guarantee all things and control all things?

The Scriptures teach that God ordains all things — both good and evil — all according to his sovereign will. Now the book uses the word *permit*, saying that God permits bad things to happen. I'm not a big fan of that word. If by *permission* we mean *ordains*, then fine. But we don't want to create two categories in our mind — things God ordained and things God merely permits. God ordains all things. So if you want to use the word *permit*, think of it as: God's permitting equals God's ordaining in the case of free agents doing things contrary to his revealed will.

Let's look at the Scriptures. Proverbs 16:9: *A man's heart plans his way, but the Lord directs his steps.* Men act, but God has ordained their actions. Proverbs 19:21: *There are many plans in a man's heart; nevertheless the Lord's counsel — that will stand.* Men act, and yet no matter what they do, God's purpose will be accomplished. Ecclesiastes 7:13: *Consider the work of God, for who can make straight what he has made crooked?* When God acts, no one can stop him. Lamentations 3:37: *Who is he who speaks and it comes to pass, when the Lord has not commanded it?*

And that's why we know we can trust God in all circumstances — because he has ordained all the good things that happen and all of the bad things that happen. If we are in dire straits, it is because the Lord who loves us — and who is doing all things for good — has ordained it to happen for us. This can be a difficult teaching to accept. But the alternative is far worse. If there are bad things happening to us because of fate, or because God is not in control, how can we derive any comfort?

The car accident you got in. The job that you lost. The cancer diagnosis. The supervisor who blames you for his failures. Stubbing your toe. Everything that happens, happens because the Lord has brought it into our lives. Matthew 10:29: *Are not two sparrows sold for a copper coin? And not one of them falls to the ground apart from your Father's will.* If a sparrow cannot fall to the ground apart from God's will, how much more the greater things of life?

And Christ could not have been crucified if it had not been granted by God. John 19:10-11: Pilate says, *Do you not know that I have power to crucify you and power to release you?* And Jesus answers: *You could have no power at all against me unless it had been given you from above.*

This doctrine should give us hope. Whether we're diagnosed with cancer or whether we experience the minor irritations of everyday life, we know it is God himself who has ordained these things, because he is a good God who works all things for good. The alternative is to resign ourselves to fate or chance or luck. That is far worse.

[A question was raised: how can God ordain evil and yet not be the author of sin?]

Sin is not like a blob of substance that was created and put into people. And the phrase "author of sin" may be an unfortunate term. What we probably should say is that God is not *culpable* for sin. He cannot tempt any man. He delights not in evil. And yet he has ordained that sinful things happen. The most sinful thing ever done was ordained by God — the death of his Son. Acts 2:23: *Him, being delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by lawless hands, have crucified, and put to death.* God ordained it; the people who crucified Christ are still responsible. That is one of those things where we bow our heads and say: we don't fully understand how this works. But we know that both are taught in the Bible.

## Point 2: God's Sovereignty Is Not Always Apparent

The second main point is that God's sovereignty is not always apparent in our life. One of the difficult things we tend to have with the idea of God's sovereignty is that we don't see him doing what we think needs to be done. We see injustices. We see wicked people prospering. We get in a car accident and wonder why God didn't prevent it.

One thing we need to understand is that God ordinarily works behind the scenes. A great example is the entire book of Esther — the one book of the Bible that does not mention the name of God, given to us so we can understand how God intervenes on behalf of his people.

In chapter 6, the turning point: Haman has been elevated and plans to kill Mordecai and all the Jews. He builds a gallows. Then one night, King Ahasuerus cannot sleep. He asks for something boring to be read to him — the chronicles of his reign. He hears the story of Mordecai revealing an assassination plot. He asks: was Mordecai ever honored for this? It turns out he wasn't. So the king decides to honor Mordecai. Haman comes in, proud as ever — and ends up being hanged on the very gallows he built for Mordecai. And the Jews are saved.

Here's what had to happen: the king couldn't sleep that night. He asked for the chronicles specifically. The reader opened to exactly that section. It just so happened that Mordecai had never been honored. It just so happened that Haman had built a gallows. And then it just so happened that the king honors Mordecai and hangs Haman.

All by chance? Or might it be that there is an Almighty God who is sovereign over the universe, working all things for good to those who love him?

The book of Ruth demonstrates the same thing. Four key events: Ruth gleans in Boaz's field — she could have chosen any field but she just happened to choose his. Boaz just happened to be coming into the field at the same time. He just happened to notice Ruth. He just happened to ask who she was. And then they get married and have children, and that preserves the Davidic line. Just ordinary, everyday events — nothing extraordinary. And yet God had ordained that those things would happen to preserve the line through which the Messiah would come.

But God also works in circumstances that from our perspective don't have happy endings. Amos 3:6: *If there is calamity in a city, will not the Lord have done it?* Ecclesiastes 7:14: *In the day of prosperity be joyful, but in the day of adversity consider: surely God has appointed the one as well as the other.* Isaiah 45:7: *I form the light and create darkness. I make peace and create calamity. I, the Lord, do all these things.*

We tend to want to shield God from being responsible for bad things. But we just read in the Bible that God says, *I do these things. I create calamity.* Amos 3:6 is still in the Bible, and we have to do something with it. Specifically, we have to believe it. God does not shy away from asserting his absolute sovereignty over all things — even the bad things.

## Point 3: God Does What He Pleases, and No One Can Thwart Him

The third main point is that God does what he pleases, and nobody can thwart him.

Job 42:2: *I know that you can do everything, and that no purpose of yours can be withheld from you.* Psalm 115:3: *But our God is in heaven; he does whatever he pleases.* Isaiah 14:27: *For the Lord of hosts has purposed, and who will annul it? His hand is stretched out, and who will turn it back?* Isaiah 43:13: *Indeed before the day was, I am he; and there is no one who can deliver out of my hand; I work, and who will reverse it?* Isaiah 46:10: *Declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things that are not yet done, saying, "My counsel shall stand, and I will do all my pleasure."* Daniel 4:35: *All the inhabitants of the earth are reputed as nothing; he does according to his will in the army of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth. No one can restrain his hand or say to him, "What have you done?"* — That is King Nebuchadnezzar, who had just been restored to sanity after being turned into a cow, essentially. And Ephesians 1:11: *In him also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will.*

All of these Scriptures show us that nobody can change what God has done. If he wants to do something, he's going to do it — no one can thwart it. And this is important for us because if God has a plan and can carry out that plan, then he can be trusted. If he cannot carry out his plans, even our prayers are futile. But thank God that he is God and that he can carry out his plan. His plan is to conform us to the image of his Son (Romans 8:29).

God's plan is as certain as his plan was for Joseph. Joseph was sold by his brothers because he was somewhat foolish — boasting about his dreams, wearing his coat of many colors, fanning the fury of his brothers. He's sold as a slave, ends up in prison, is forgotten for two years. And as a result of all this suffering, the people of Israel are saved. They're brought into Egypt, become a great people, and we know the rest of the story.

When we are in the midst of dark days — just like Joseph — we can count on God to work it for our good. God was never surprised by what happened to Joseph. When the doctor tells you that you have cancer at stage four, God is not saying, "I didn't know about that." God doesn't say, "If only I had known, I would have changed it."

## Application: Trusting God in Adversity

Trusting God happens one event at a time, one circumstance at a time, one hour at a time — sometimes one minute at a time. We need to remind ourselves to trust God. And trusting God is not a matter of feelings. It is a matter of the will.

Think about when Christ was in the Garden of Gethsemane. On that night he is betrayed and he knows what he's going to go through. He is in excruciating distress — so much that he sweats blood. And he prays to his Father that this cup would pass from him. And yet he is also able to pray: *Not my will, but yours be done.* The Lord Jesus Christ was trusting in God's sovereignty. He didn't want to go through the cross. He asked to not go through the cross. And yet he was also able to say: not my will but yours be done. That is what trusting God's sovereignty looks like.

When we face adversity, our typical response is: how do I get out of this as fast as possible? That's our natural human response. And it's not wrong to want to be out of adversity — the Psalms are full of prayers for deliverance. But that should not be our number one priority. Our number one priority should be to learn how to trust God in the midst of that adversity. How can we become thankful when going through difficulties? How can we glorify God while walking through the valley of the shadow of death?

Too often our attitude is to get relief from our problems rather than to learn from them. And yet — how many times have you gone through something really, really difficult and learned so much about the Lord in that time? Usually in the darkest valleys, that's when we learn the most about who God is and how much he loves us. Sometimes we have to be thrown into the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual to experience how God protects us from the flames.

And as an aside: if you are unthankful, it's because you are not trusting God's sovereignty. 1 Thessalonians 5:18 commands us: *Give thanks in everything, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you.*

So study the Scriptures. Meditate on them. Ask the Lord to help you understand as much as you possibly can about his sovereignty. And let the Lord build up your faith in him.

Let us pray.

Thank you, good and loving, merciful God. Thank you that you have indeed ordained all things according to your purpose, and you have ordained all these things for our good. We ask you, Lord, to humble us under your Word as we look at your sovereignty. We pray that you would build up our faith and help us trust you, for you are indeed our Savior, you are our God, and you do work all things for our good. We ask you to bless the rest of our day, bless the preaching of your Word in the services to follow. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
